description: Bengali LLM Benchmark: Comprehensive evaluation of 10 recent open-source LLMs across 8 translated Bengali datasets spanning Commonsense (Hellaswag, Winogrande, CommonsenseQA, BoolQ, OpenBookQA), Science (ARC), Math (GSM8K), and Multidomain (MMLU) categories. 20 major English benchmarks translated using GPT-4o-mini with systematic validation. Bengali performance consistently lags English with notable gaps for smaller models and Mistral family. DeepSeek shows highest cross-language robustness. Tokenization analysis reveals Bengali requires 2-7x more tokens per word than English, with negative correlation between excessive tokenization and model accuracy.

description: IndicEval-XL: Comprehensive multilingual code generation benchmark for 6 Indic languages (Hindi, Tamil, Urdu, Marathi, Punjabi, Sanskrit) with 12 programming languages (Python, Java, Go, Kotlin, PHP, Ruby, Scala, JavaScript, C#, Perl, Swift, TypeScript), creating 6,720 coding problems total. Built from HumanEval-XL dataset using IndicTrans2 for translation with rigorous quality checks (BERTScore >0.95). Uses CodeBERTScore for evaluation rather than pass@k to avoid penalizing valid solutions with naming variations. Gemini models achieve scores 0.879-0.946, LLaMA 7B scores 0.794-0.870. JavaScript, Python, PHP show highest alignment scores (>0.930), while Sanskrit poses greatest challenge (0.794-0.917) due to limited training data. Languages represent 14% of world population.

description: HumanEval-XL: Largest multilingual code generation benchmark with 22,080 prompts across 23 natural languages and 12 programming languages (Python, Java, Go, Kotlin, PHP, Ruby, Scala, JavaScript, C#, Perl, Swift, TypeScript). Built from Multilingual HumanEval using GPT-4 translation with BERTScore quality control (>0.95 threshold). Covers 11 language families with parallel data enabling fair cross-lingual evaluation. GPT-4 achieves highest performance, CodeGen2 outperforms GPT-3.5 in most languages except Python. Python most effectively tackled, while JavaScript, C#, Perl, Swift, TypeScript prove challenging. Performance correlation within model families regardless of size (Pearson: CodeGen2 0.8, GPT 0.87). Languages from Afro-Asiatic, Turkic families show lower performance.

models: o1-preview, o1-mini, GPT-4, GPT-4o, Llama 3.1 405B, Llama 3.1 70B, Llama 3.1 8B, Gemini-1.5 pro, Gemini-1.5 flash, Claude-3.5 Sonnet, Claude-3-Sonnet, Claude-3-Haiku, Qwen-2.5 72B, Mistral Large 2
description: Multi-IF extends IFEval into a challenging multi-turn and multilingual instruction following benchmark with 4,501 conversations across 8 languages. Each conversation contains 3 turns with verifiable instructions, revealing significant performance degradation as turns increase (e.g., o1-preview drops from 0.877 to 0.707 accuracy). The benchmark shows lower performance on non-Latin script languages (Hindi, Russian, Chinese) and identifies instruction forgetting as a key challenge, with o1 models showing superior error correction capabilities through hidden chain-of-thought reasoning.

The benchmark shows lower performance on non-Latin script languages (Hindi, Russian, Chinese) and identifies instruction forgetting as a key challenge, with o1 models showing superior error correction capabilities through hidden chain-of-thought reasoning.

description: BFCL-Hi: Hindi Function Calling Benchmark evaluates Hindi LLMs' ability to understand Hindi queries and map them to English function definitions. Part of comprehensive Hindi LLM evaluation suite with 2,251 samples using GCP translation with hybrid approach - Hindi conversational history with English function schemas. Tests simple, multiple, parallel function calls plus relevance/irrelevance categories. Evaluated using BFCL Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) methodology. Nemotron-4-Mini-Hindi-4B-Instruct leads significantly on BFCL-Hi, demonstrating value of language-specific training.

Description: BFCL-Hi: Hindi Function Calling Benchmark evaluates Hindi LLMs' ability to understand Hindi queries and map them to English function definitions. Part of comprehensive Hindi LLM evaluation suite with 2,251 samples using GCP translation with hybrid approach - Hindi conversational history with English function schemas. Tests simple, multiple, parallel function calls plus relevance/irrelevance categories. Evaluated using BFCL Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) methodology. Nemotron-4-Mini-Hindi-4B-Instruct leads significantly on BFCL-Hi, demonstrating value of language-specific training.

description: MT-Bench-X: Multilingual translation of MT-Bench into parallel benchmark across 5 Indo-European languages. Framework evaluates multilingual instruction-following capabilities with 400 two-turn examples (80 per language). Uses DeepL translation with human quality assurance (31-72 manual corrections per language). Evaluates 8 categories: Writing, Roleplay, Reasoning, Math, Coding, Extraction, STEM, Humanities. Results demonstrate instruction-tuning on parallel datasets benefits cross-lingual instruction following by up to 9.9%. Challenges Superficial Alignment Hypothesis for mid-sized models. Human evaluation shows 39% agreement between humans and GPT-4 as judge, with fair inter-annotator agreement (κ=0.37).

description: IndicGenBench: A comprehensive multilingual benchmark evaluating LLM generation capabilities across 29 Indic languages spanning 13 scripts and 4 language families. Contains 5 user-facing tasks: cross-lingual summarization (CrossSum-In), machine translation (Flores-In), multilingual QA (XQuAD-In), and cross-lingual QA (XorQA-In-Xx, XorQA-In-En). Features professionally translated human-curated data with multi-way parallel examples. Comprehensive evaluation shows significant performance gaps between English and Indic languages across all models, with PaLM-2-L achieving best results but substantial room for improvement. Provides first-ever evaluation datasets for 18 languages in some tasks. Includes training/validation data for fine-tuning and in-context learning experiments.

description: Qwen3 Technical Report presents the latest version of Qwen model family with dense and Mixture-of-Expert (MoE) architectures ranging from 0.6 to 235 billion parameters. Key innovation includes integration of thinking mode (complex multi-step reasoning) and non-thinking mode (rapid context-driven responses) in a unified framework with dynamic mode switching and thinking budget mechanism for adaptive computational resource allocation. Achieves state-of-the-art results across diverse benchmarks including code generation, mathematical reasoning, and agent tasks. Expands multilingual support from 29 to 119 languages and dialects compared to Qwen2.5.

description: Comprehensive empirical evaluation of 20 open-source Small Language Models (SLMs) ranging from 0.4B to 10B parameters on five diverse code-related benchmarks (HumanEval, MBPP, Mercury, HumanEvalPack, and CodeXGLUE). Assesses functional correctness, computational efficiency, and multilingual programming language performance. Reveals that compact SLMs achieve competitive results while maintaining performance-efficiency balance, with 10% performance improvements requiring nearly 4x increase in VRAM consumption. Statistical analysis shows generalizability across programming languages despite performance variations.

Statistical analysis shows generalizability across programming languages despite performance variations.

description: IndicEval-XL: Comprehensive multilingual code generation benchmark for 6 Indic languages (Hindi, Tamil, Urdu, Marathi, Punjabi, Sanskrit) with 12 programming languages (Python, Java, Go, Kotlin, PHP, Ruby, Scala, JavaScript, C#, Perl, Swift, TypeScript), creating 6,720 coding problems total. Built from HumanEval-XL dataset using IndicTrans2 for translation with rigorous quality checks (BERTScore >0.95). description: HumanEval-XL: Largest multilingual code generation benchmark with 22,080 prompts across 23 natural languages and 12 programming languages (Python, Java, Go, Kotlin, PHP, Ruby, Scala, JavaScript, C#, Perl, Swift, TypeScript). Built from Multilingual HumanEval using GPT-4 translation with BERTScore quality control (>0.95 threshold). Covers 11 language families with parallel data enabling fair cross-lingual evaluation. GPT-4 achieves highest performance, CodeGen2 outperforms GPT-3.5 in most languages except Python. Python most effectively tackled, while JavaScript, C#, Perl, Swift, TypeScript prove challenging. Performance correlation within model families regardless of size (Pearson: CodeGen2 0.8, GPT 0.87). Languages from Afro-Asiatic, Turkic families show lower performance.

description: Multi-IF extends IFEval into a challenging multi-turn and multilingual instruction following benchmark with 4,501 conversations across 8 languages. Each conversation contains 3 turns with verifiable instructions, revealing significant performance degradation as turns increase (e.g., o1-preview drops from 0.877 to 0.707 accuracy). The benchmark shows lower performance on non-Latin script languages (Hindi, Russian, Chinese) and identifies instruction forgetting as a key challenge, with o1 models showing superior error correction capabilities through hidden chain-of-thought reasoning.

description: MT-Bench-X: Multilingual translation of MT-Bench into parallel benchmark across 5 Indo-European languages. Framework evaluates multilingual instruction-following capabilities with 400 two-turn examples (80 per language). Uses DeepL translation with human quality assurance (31-72 manual corrections per language). Evaluates 8 categories: Writing, Roleplay, Reasoning, Math, Coding, Extraction, STEM, Humanities. Results demonstrate instruction-tuning on parallel datasets benefits cross-lingual instruction following by up to 9.9%. Challenges Superficial Alignment Hypothesis for mid-sized models. Human evaluation shows 39% agreement between humans and GPT-4 as judge, with fair inter-annotator agreement (κ=0.37).

description: MGSM is a multilingual grade-school math benchmark created by manually translating 250 grade-school math problems from the GSM8K dataset into ten typologically diverse languages including English, Bengali, Chinese, French, German, Japanese, Russian, Spanish, Swahili, Telugu, and Thai. The benchmark evaluates the reasoning abilities of large language models in multilingual settings using chain-of-thought prompting. Results show that multilingual reasoning abilities emerge with increasing model scale, and models demonstrate strikingly strong multilingual reasoning abilities even in underrepresented languages such as Bengali and Swahili. The benchmark extends multilingual reasoning evaluation to commensense reasoning and word-in-context semantic judgment tasks.

description: MetaMathQA is a dataset for fine-tuning language models to specialize in mathematical reasoning. The approach bootstraps mathematical questions by rewriting problems from multiple perspectives without requiring extra knowledge, creating a comprehensive training dataset. MetaMath fine-tuned models significantly outperform existing open-source LLMs on popular mathematical reasoning benchmarks like GSM8K and MATH. The MetaMath-7B model achieves 66.4% on GSM8K and 19.4% on MATH, while MetaMath-70B reaches 82.3% on GSM8K, demonstrating substantial improvements in mathematical problem-solving capabilities through specialized training on rewritten mathematical problems.

description: PolyMath is a comprehensive multilingual mathematical reasoning benchmark covering 18 languages and 4 easy-to-hard difficulty levels. The benchmark ensures difficulty comprehensiveness, language diversity, and high-quality translation, making it highly discriminative for evaluating reasoning LLMs in multilingual contexts. Evaluation reveals that even advanced models like Qwen-3-235B-A22B-Thinking and Gemini-2.5-pro achieve only 54.6 and 52.2 benchmark scores respectively. The benchmark identifies key challenges including wide performance variation across languages, low input-output language consistency in reasoning models, and significant differences in thinking length by language, providing insights for improving multilingual mathematical reasoning capabilities.

description: MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding) is a comprehensive test measuring text models' multitask accuracy across 57 tasks including elementary mathematics, US history, computer science, law, and more. The math reasoning subset focuses specifically on mathematical problem-solving capabilities. To achieve high accuracy, models must possess extensive world knowledge and strong problem-solving abilities. The benchmark reveals that while most recent models have near random-chance accuracy, the largest models show significant improvements. However, substantial improvements are still needed across all tasks to reach expert-level accuracy, making it a challenging and discriminative evaluation for mathematical reasoning capabilities.

description: Despite the growing importance of Arabic as a global language, there is a notable lack of language models pre-trained exclusively on Arabic data. This shortage has led to limited benchmarks available for assessing language model performance in Arabic. To address this gap, we introduce two novel benchmarks designed to evaluate models' mathematical reasoning and language understanding abilities in Arabic. These benchmarks are derived from a General Aptitude Test (GAT) called Qiyas exam, a standardized test widely used for university admissions in Saudi Arabia. For validation purposes, we assess the performance of ChatGPT-3.5-turbo and ChatGPT-4 on our benchmarks. Our findings reveal that these benchmarks pose a significant challenge, with ChatGPT-4 achieving an overall average accuracy of 64%, while ChatGPT-3.5-turbo achieved an overall accuracy of 49% across the various question types in the Qiyas benchmark. We believe the release of these benchmarks will pave the way for enhancing the mathematical reasoning and language understanding capabilities of future models tailored for the low-resource Arabic language.
embedding_text:
Description: Despite the growing importance of Arabic as a global language, there is a notable lack of language models pre-trained exclusively on Arabic data. This shortage has led to limited benchmarks available for assessing language model performance in Arabic. To address this gap, we introduce two novel benchmarks designed to evaluate models' mathematical reasoning and language understanding abilities in Arabic. These benchmarks are derived from a General Aptitude Test (GAT) called Qiyas exam, a standardized test widely used for university admissions in Saudi Arabia. For validation purposes, we assess the performance of ChatGPT-3.5-turbo and ChatGPT-4 on our benchmarks. Our findings reveal that these benchmarks pose a significant challenge, with ChatGPT-4 achieving an overall average accuracy of 64%, while ChatGPT-3.5-turbo achieved an overall accuracy of 49% across the various question types in the Qiyas benchmark. We believe the release of these benchmarks will pave the way for enhancing the mathematical reasoning and language understanding capabilities of future models tailored for the low-resource Arabic language.

description: IndiMathQA covers math word problems from India's NCERT curriculum with native and GPT-generated questions. HAWP features curated arithmetic word problems analyzing accuracy on basic operations. Large Language Models (LLMs) excel in linguistic tasks but struggle with mathematical reasoning, particularly in non-English languages like Hindi. This research aims to enhance the mathematical reasoning skills of smaller, resource-efficient open-source LLMs in both Hindi and English using zero-shot, few-shot chain-of-thought (CoT) methods, and supervised fine-tuning. The approach incorporates curriculum learning, progressively training models on increasingly difficult problems, a novel Decomposition Strategy to simplify complex arithmetic operations, and a Structured Solution Design that divides solutions into phases. WizardMath 7B exceeds Gemini's accuracy on English datasets by +6% and matches Gemini's performance on Hindi datasets. Both evaluate Hindi LLMs with curriculum learning approaches showing WizardMath-7B can approach GPT-4/Gemini Pro performance in Hindi math. Emphasizes benefits of bilingual datasets.

description: OlymMATH is a challenging Olympiad-level mathematical reasoning benchmark featuring 200 meticulously curated problems, each manually verified and available in parallel English and Chinese versions. The benchmark spans four core mathematical fields (algebra, geometry, number theory, combinatorics) and is organized into AIME-level problems (easy) and significantly more challenging problems (hard). Problems feature verifiable numerical solutions for objective evaluation. State-of-the-art models demonstrate notably limited accuracy on the hard subset, with DeepSeek-R1 achieving 19.5%, o3-mini 31.2%, and Gemini 2.5 Pro Exp 58.4% on OlymMATH-EN-HARD, highlighting the benchmark's discriminative power and the continued challenge of Olympiad-level mathematical reasoning.

description: CMATH is a Chinese Elementary School Math Word Problems dataset comprising 1,700 elementary school-level math word problems with detailed annotations, sourced from actual Chinese workbooks and exams. The dataset covers all six elementary school grades (Grades 1-6) and aims to assess the grade level of elementary school math corresponding to popular large language models' abilities. Only GPT-4 achieves success (accuracy ≥60%) across all six elementary school grades, while other models falter at different grade levels. The dataset also includes robustness testing with distracting information augmented to original problems, where GPT-4 maintains robustness while other models fail.

description: Three Italian-native benchmarks for evaluating mathematical and linguistic understanding: Invalsi MATE (420 questions) for mathematical reasoning based on standardized Italian school tests, Invalsi ITA (1264 questions) for language understanding, and Olimpiadi MATE (619 questions) for advanced mathematical reasoning from Italian Math Olympics. Tests span grades 1-18 (ages 6-18) with four question types (multiple choice, true-false/binary, number, fill-gap). Results show Llama 3.1 70B instruct achieves 71% on Invalsi MATE (only model outperforming Italian students), 88% on Invalsi ITA, and Llama 3.1 405B instruct reaches 45% on challenging Olimpiadi MATE. Multilingual models outperform English-first models even with fewer parameters, and fine-tuning on Italian cannot fill the performance gap created by English-only pre-training.

description: MultiMWP is a multi-way parallel corpus of Math Word Problems (MWPs) in nine languages, including six low-resource languages. It is the largest multilingual MWP dataset available, demonstrating the viability of using large language models for autoregressive MWP generation in both monolingual and multilingual setups, particularly for low-resource languages. The dataset integrates a math constraint satisfaction module with autoregressive text generation. Evaluations reveal that factors affecting generation include language representation in the LLM, model size, existence of similar languages in the model, and language script. Results show autoregressive MWP generation on LLMs is promising even for low-resource languages.

description: Tamil-Llama is a new Tamil language model based on LLaMA 2, enhanced with an addition of 16,000 Tamil tokens to achieve superior text generation and comprehension in the Tamil language. The model employs LoRA methodology for efficient training on a comprehensive Tamil corpus, ensuring computational feasibility and model robustness. The work introduces a Tamil-translated version of the Alpaca dataset and a subset of the OpenOrca dataset tailored for instruction fine-tuning. Results showcase significant performance improvements in Tamil text generation, with potential implications for the broader landscape of LLMs in Indian languages. While no direct mathematical reasoning benchmarks are provided in this work, it establishes the foundation for future Tamil mathematical reasoning evaluations.

description: Investigation of the interaction between linguistic and mathematical reasoning in language models using multilingual number puzzles. The study examines how numeral systems vary across languages in constructing and combining numbers. While humans consistently learn to navigate numeral diversity, LLMs struggle with linguistic-mathematical puzzles involving cross-linguistic numeral systems. The research establishes that models cannot consistently solve such problems unless mathematical operations are explicitly marked using known symbols (+, ×, etc.). Humans use linguistic understanding of numbers to make inferences about implicit compositional structure of numerals, while LLMs lack this notion of implicit numeral structure. The ability to flexibly infer compositional rules from implicit patterns in human-scale data remains an open challenge for current reasoning models.

models: GPT-3.5 Turbo
description: YouTube Comments Decoded: A study leveraging LLMs for low-resource language classification focusing on sarcasm detection in code-mixed social media content. The work introduces a novel gold standard corpus for sarcasm and sentiment detection within code-mixed texts, specifically Tamil-English and Malayalam-English languages. The primary objective is to identify sarcasm and sentiment polarity within code-mixed datasets collected from social media platforms. Using state-of-the-art large language models like GPT-3.5 Turbo via prompting, the study achieved a macro-F1 score of 0.61 for Tamil language and 0.50 for Malayalam language in classifying comments into sarcastic or non-sarcastic categories. While not directly mathematical reasoning, sarcasm detection requires logical inference and understanding of implicit meaning, serving as a proxy for reasoning capabilities in low-resource languages.

description: PolyMath is a comprehensive multilingual mathematical reasoning benchmark covering 18 languages and 4 easy-to-hard difficulty levels. The benchmark ensures difficulty comprehensiveness, language diversity, and high-quality translation, making it highly discriminative for evaluating reasoning LLMs in multilingual contexts. Evaluation reveals that even advanced models like Qwen-3-235B-A22B-Thinking and Gemini-2.5-pro achieve only 54.6 and 52.2 benchmark scores respectively. The benchmark identifies key challenges including wide performance variation across languages, low input-output language consistency in reasoning models, and significant differences in thinking length by language, providing insights for improving multilingual mathematical reasoning capabilities.

models: ChatGPT-3.5-turbo, ChatGPT-4, Gemini Pro
description: Despite the growing importance of Arabic as a global language, there is a notable lack of language models pre-trained exclusively on Arabic data. This shortage has led to limited benchmarks available for assessing language model performance in Arabic. To address this gap, we introduce two novel benchmarks designed to evaluate models' mathematical reasoning and language understanding abilities in Arabic. These benchmarks are derived from a General Aptitude Test (GAT) called Qiyas exam, a standardized test widely used for university admissions in Saudi Arabia. For validation purposes, we assess the performance of ChatGPT-3.5-turbo and ChatGPT-4 on our benchmarks. Our findings reveal that these benchmarks pose a significant challenge, with ChatGPT-4 achieving an overall average accuracy of 64%, while ChatGPT-3.5-turbo achieved an overall accuracy of 49% across the various question types in the Qiyas benchmark. We believe the release of these benchmarks will pave the way for enhancing the mathematical reasoning and language understanding capabilities of future models tailored for the low-resource Arabic language.
embedding_text:
Description: Despite the growing importance of Arabic as a global language, there is a notable lack of language models pre-trained exclusively on Arabic data. This shortage has led to limited benchmarks available for assessing language model performance in Arabic. To address this gap, we introduce two novel benchmarks designed to evaluate models' mathematical reasoning and language understanding abilities in Arabic. These benchmarks are derived from a General Aptitude Test (GAT) called Qiyas exam, a standardized test widely used for university admissions in Saudi Arabia. For validation purposes, we assess the performance of ChatGPT-3.5-turbo and ChatGPT-4 on our benchmarks. Our findings reveal that these benchmarks pose a significant challenge, with ChatGPT-4 achieving an overall average accuracy of 64%, while ChatGPT-3.5-turbo achieved an overall accuracy of 49% across the various question types in the Qiyas benchmark. We believe the release of these benchmarks will pave the way for enhancing the mathematical reasoning and language understanding capabilities of future models tailored for the low-resource Arabic language.

description: IndiMathQA covers math word problems from India's NCERT curriculum with native and GPT-generated questions. HAWP features curated arithmetic word problems analyzing accuracy on basic operations. Large Language Models (LLMs) excel in linguistic tasks but struggle with mathematical reasoning, particularly in non-English languages like Hindi. This research aims to enhance the mathematical reasoning skills of smaller, resource-efficient open-source LLMs in both Hindi and English using zero-shot, few-shot chain-of-thought (CoT) methods, and supervised fine-tuning. The approach incorporates curriculum learning, progressively training models on increasingly difficult problems, a novel Decomposition Strategy to simplify complex arithmetic operations, and a Structured Solution Design that divides solutions into phases. WizardMath 7B exceeds Gemini's accuracy on English datasets by +6% and matches Gemini's performance on Hindi datasets. Both evaluate Hindi LLMs with curriculum learning approaches showing WizardMath-7B can approach GPT-4/Gemini Pro performance in Hindi math. Emphasizes benefits of bilingual datasets.

description: OlymMATH is a challenging Olympiad-level mathematical reasoning benchmark featuring 200 meticulously curated problems, each manually verified and available in parallel English and Chinese versions. The benchmark spans four core mathematical fields (algebra, geometry, number theory, combinatorics) and is organized into AIME-level problems (easy) and significantly more challenging problems (hard). Problems feature verifiable numerical solutions for objective evaluation. State-of-the-art models demonstrate notably limited accuracy on the hard subset, with DeepSeek-R1 achieving 19.5%, o3-mini 31.2%, and Gemini 2.5 Pro Exp 58.4% on OlymMATH-EN-HARD, highlighting the benchmark's discriminative power and the continued challenge of Olympiad-level mathematical reasoning.

models: Llama 3.1 70B/405B instruct, Llama 2 70B chat, Llama 3 8B/70B instruct, Mixtral-8x7B instruct, Mistral-7B instruct, Camoscio 2 70B instruct, LLamantino 2 70B chat, ANITA 8B DPO, Minerva 3B
description: Three Italian-native benchmarks for evaluating mathematical and linguistic understanding: Invalsi MATE (420 questions) for mathematical reasoning based on standardized Italian school tests, Invalsi ITA (1264 questions) for language understanding, and Olimpiadi MATE (619 questions) for advanced mathematical reasoning from Italian Math Olympics. Tests span grades 1-18 (ages 6-18) with four question types (multiple choice, true-false/binary, number, fill-gap). Results show Llama 3.1 70B instruct achieves 71% on Invalsi MATE (only model outperforming Italian students), 88% on Invalsi ITA, and Llama 3.1 405B instruct reaches 45% on challenging Olimpiadi MATE. Multilingual models outperform English-first models even with fewer parameters, and fine-tuning on Italian cannot fill the performance gap created by English-only pre-training.

description: TyDi QA is a question answering dataset covering 11 typologically diverse languages with 204K question-answer pairs. The benchmark is designed for information-seeking tasks where questions are written by people who want to know the answer but don't know it yet. Data is collected directly in each language without translation to avoid priming effects. The languages are selected for their typological diversity to test model generalization across a wide range of linguistic features, making it a challenging evaluation for multilingual QA systems. TyDiQA-GoldP is a multilingual QA dataset, supporting Urdu, widely used for zero-shot cross-lingual LLM evaluation.

description: MLQA is a multi-way aligned extractive QA evaluation benchmark designed to spur research in cross-lingual question answering. It contains QA instances in 7 languages (English, Arabic, German, Spanish, Hindi, Vietnamese, and Simplified Chinese) with over 12K QA instances in English and 5K in each other language. Each QA instance is parallel between 4 languages on average. Built using a novel alignment context strategy on Wikipedia articles, MLQA serves as a cross-lingual extension to existing extractive QA datasets, enabling evaluation of cross-lingual transfer capabilities without requiring training data in target languages.

description: XQuAD (Cross-lingual Question Answering Dataset) is a comprehensive cross-lingual benchmark comprising 240 paragraphs and 1190 question-answer pairs from SQuAD v1.1 translated into ten languages by professional translators. The dataset was created to evaluate cross-lingual transferability of monolingual representations and demonstrates that deep monolingual models can learn abstractions that generalize across languages without requiring shared vocabulary or joint training. XQuAD serves as a more comprehensive evaluation tool for testing cross-lingual question answering capabilities.

description: MKQA (Multilingual Knowledge Questions and Answers) is an open-domain question answering evaluation set comprising 10k question-answer pairs aligned across 26 typologically diverse languages (260k question-answer pairs in total). Answers are based on a heavily curated, language-independent data representation, making results comparable across languages and independent of language-specific passages. With 26 languages, this dataset provides the widest range of languages for evaluating question answering and is particularly challenging for low-resource languages. The benchmark evaluates both generative and extractive QA methods in zero-shot and translation settings.

description: MedExpQA is the first multilingual benchmark based on medical exams to evaluate LLMs in Medical Question Answering. The benchmark includes reference gold explanations written by medical doctors, enabling evaluation of both answer accuracy and reasoning quality. Available in four languages (English, Spanish, French, Italian), it addresses the neglected area of non-English medical QA evaluation. The benchmark evaluates various medical LLMs including LLaMA2, Mistral, PMC-LLaMA, and BioMistral, revealing significant room for improvement especially in non-English languages and highlighting challenges in integrating medical knowledge for downstream QA tasks.

description: VLR-Bench is a multilingual visual question answering (VQA) benchmark for evaluating vision language models (VLMs) based on retrieval augmented generation (RAG). Unlike existing external knowledge-based VQA datasets, VLR-Bench includes five input passages to test models' ability to determine which passage is useful for answering queries. The benchmark includes 32,000 automatically generated instruction-following examples (VLR-IF) designed to enhance RAG capabilities of VLMs. Evaluated on state-of-the-art models like Llava-Llama-3, it provides comprehensive assessment of multilingual visual question answering with retrieval augmentation across English, Korean, and Chinese.

HindiBERT achieves best Hindi performance (47.84% EM), MahaBERT best for Marathi (42.97% EM). Uses novel sliding window technique for answer alignment in translated contexts. Demonstrates monolingual advantage over multilingual models for low-resource Indian languages.

description: Largest multilingual benchmark for evaluating text generation capabilities of LLMs on 29 Indic languages spanning 4 language families (Indo-European, Dravidian, Sino-Tibetan, Austroasiatic) and 13 scripts. Five user-facing tasks: CrossSum-In (cross-lingual summarization), Flores-In (machine translation), XQuAD-In (multilingual QA), XorQA-In-Xx and XorQA-In-En (cross-lingual QA). All datasets manually translated by professional annotators ensuring high quality. Languages classified by resourcefulness: Higher (9), Medium (7), Low (13). PaLM-2-L achieves best performance but significant gap remains between English and Indic languages. Cross-lingual QA shows particularly challenging results with models performing better at understanding than generation in low-resource languages. Comprehensive evaluation reveals tokenization disparities affecting performance, with fertility ranging from 4.1 (Pashto) to 19.9 (Tibetan). First-ever evaluation data for 18 Indic languages in generation tasks.

description: Benchmarking Hindi LLMs: A New Suite of Datasets and a Comparative Analysis - introduces five comprehensive Hindi evaluation benchmarks for instruction-tuned LLMs: IFEval-Hi (848 samples), MT-Bench-Hi (200 samples), GSM8K-Hi (1319 samples), ChatRAG-Hi (5948 samples), and BFCL-Hi (2251 samples). Gemma-2-9b-it leads SLMs, GPT-OSS-120B leads LLMs. Demonstrates specialized language training advantage.

description: HindiLLM: Large Language Model for Hindi - introduces two autoregressive Hindi language models (HindiLLM-Small: 124M parameters, HindiLLM-Medium: 355M parameters) trained from scratch on 37.34GB Hindi corpus. Custom HindiLLM tokenizer with 50K vocabulary reduces token fertility by 50% compared to GPT-2. Evaluated on 7 downstream tasks: sentiment analysis, text classification, NLI, CSQA, WSTP, discourse classification, machine translation. HindiLLM-Medium outperforms GPT-3.5 Turbo and other baselines across most tasks. Achieves 81.04% accuracy on IITP-Movie sentiment, 98.6% F1 on NLI.

description: MMCricBench-3K: Mind the Language Gap - Visual Question Answering on cricket scorecards evaluating Large Vision-Language Models on complex numerical and cross-lingual reasoning over semi-structured tabular images. Dataset comprises 1,463 synthetically generated scorecard images with 1,500 English QA pairs across three categories: Direct Retrieval (C1), Basic Arithmetic (C2), Multi-step Reasoning (C3). MMCricBench-E-1.5K (English) and MMCricBench-H-1.5K (Hindi) subsets reveal significant cross-lingual performance gaps. GPT-4o achieves best performance (57.3% English, 45.1% Hindi), demonstrating persistent challenges in structure-aware visual text understanding and cross-script generalization.

description: A Unified Framework for Multilingual and Code-Mixed Visual Question Answering (Gupta et al., AACL 2020) - First VQA framework for Hindi, English, and Code-mixed (Hinglish) languages. Introduces hierarchy of shared layers controlled by attention-based soft layer sharing mechanism. Uses bi-linear attention with residual connection to fuse language and image features. Creates Hindi and Code-mixed VQA datasets by exploiting linguistic properties. Achieves state-of-the-art performance across English, Hindi, and Code-mixed settings, demonstrating foundational experimental setup for Indic VQA with explicit Devanagari/code-mix capabilities.

description: MTVQA: Benchmarking Multilingual Text-Centric Visual Question Answering - First multilingual text-centric VQA benchmark with native human expert annotations across 9 languages covering 8,794 images and 28,607 QA pairs. Addresses visual-textual misalignment problem in multilingual text-centric scenarios. Comprehensive evaluation of state-of-the-art MLLMs including closed-source (GPT-4o, GPT-4V, Gemini, Claude3) and open-source models (Qwen2-VL, InternVL, etc.) reveals significant performance gaps compared to human baseline (30.9% vs 79.7% for top model). Demonstrates potential for multilingual training data to enhance TEC-VQA capabilities.

Comprehensive evaluation of state-of-the-art MLLMs including closed-source (GPT-4o, GPT-4V, Gemini, Claude3) and open-source models (Qwen2-VL, InternVL, etc.) reveals significant performance gaps compared to human baseline (30.9% vs 79.7% for top model). description: M2QA: Multi-domain Multilingual Question Answering - First multi-domain multilingual extractive QA benchmark covering three typologically diverse languages (German, Turkish, Chinese) and three distinct domains (product reviews, news, creative writing) with 13,500 human-annotated instances. Evaluates joint language and domain transfer across XLM-R baselines, LLMs (GPT-3.5, Aya-23, Llama 2&3), and adapter-based setups (MAD-X+Domain, MAD-X2). Reveals significant performance gaps between LLMs and fine-tuned models, demonstrating challenges in cross-lingual cross-domain generalization.

description: Novel benchmark evaluating 8 cutting-edge multimodal large language models on visual reasoning tasks including difference spotting and diagram interpretation. Integrates multi-image reasoning with rejection-based evaluation and positional bias detection using entropy metrics. ChatGPT-o1 leads with 82.5% overall accuracy and 70.0% rejection accuracy, followed by Gemini 2.0 Flash Experimental at 70.8%. Study reveals model size is not sole performance determinant and establishes new standard for multimodal LLM evaluation

description: Large-scale culturally diverse multilingual VQA benchmark covering 10,374 questions across 30 countries and 31 languages with 13 scripts. Created through grassroots crowdsourcing with native speakers and cultural experts. Features 10 categories: Vehicles, Cooking/Food, People/Everyday Life, Sports, Plants/Animals, Objects/Clothing, Brands, Geography/Landmarks, Tradition/Art/History, Public Figures/Pop Culture. Includes both personal and web-sourced images with cultural relevance validation. Demonstrates significant performance challenges for current MLLMs, especially in local languages. GPT-4o achieves highest accuracy (75.4% English, 74.3% local), while open models like LLaVA-1.5-7B achieve 49.6% English, 35.5% local languages. Shows substantial performance gaps between closed and open-source models, with particular difficulties in cooking/food and pop culture categories.

description: Largest publicly available multilingual context-grounded question-answering dataset for 11 major Indian languages from two language families. Encompasses both extractive and abstractive QA tasks incorporating existing datasets and English QA datasets translated into Indian languages. Synthetic dataset generated using Gemini model with manual verification. Combines Hindi SQuAD (5000 instances), XQuAD (1190 QA pairs), ChaII Dataset (1104 questions), IndicQA dataset (cloze-style reading comprehension), MLQA (4918 triples), MS MARCO (55k instances), NQ-Open, XORQA, LLaMA Index. Translate-Test paradigm significantly outperforms multilingual LLMs in low-resource languages. Evaluation using F1 scores for extractive QA and ROUGE-L for abstractive QA. Gemma2-Base performed best across most languages. BloomZ showed highest improvement for instruction-tuned models. Performance varies by resource availability: mid-resource languages (Hindi, Bengali) benefit from multilingual models while low-resource languages (Punjabi, Gujarati, Odia) perform better with translation-based approaches.

description: MLQA is a multi-way aligned extractive QA evaluation benchmark designed to spur research in cross-lingual question answering. It contains QA instances in 7 languages (English, Arabic, German, Spanish, Hindi, Vietnamese, and Simplified Chinese) with over 12K QA instances in English and 5K in each other language. Each QA instance is parallel between 4 languages on average. Built using a novel alignment context strategy on Wikipedia articles, MLQA serves as a cross-lingual extension to existing extractive QA datasets, enabling evaluation of cross-lingual transfer capabilities without requiring training data in target languages.

description: XTREME (Cross-lingual TRansfer Evaluation of Multilingual Encoders) is a massively multilingual multi-task benchmark for evaluating cross-lingual generalization capabilities across 40 languages and 9 tasks. The benchmark includes question answering as one of its core tasks, enabling comprehensive evaluation of multilingual models on QA across a diverse and representative set of languages. XTREME demonstrates significant performance gaps between English and cross-lingual transferred models, particularly on syntactic and sentence retrieval tasks, and reveals wide performance spreads across different languages.

description: MedExpQA is the first multilingual benchmark based on medical exams to evaluate LLMs in Medical Question Answering. The benchmark includes reference gold explanations written by medical doctors, enabling evaluation of both answer accuracy and reasoning quality. Available in four languages (English, Spanish, French, Italian), it addresses the neglected area of non-English medical QA evaluation. The benchmark evaluates various medical LLMs including LLaMA2, Mistral, PMC-LLaMA, and BioMistral, revealing significant room for improvement especially in non-English languages and highlighting challenges in integrating medical knowledge for downstream QA tasks.

description: VLR-Bench is a multilingual visual question answering (VQA) benchmark for evaluating vision language models (VLMs) based on retrieval augmented generation (RAG). Unlike existing external knowledge-based VQA datasets, VLR-Bench includes five input passages to test models' ability to determine which passage is useful for answering queries. The benchmark includes 32,000 automatically generated instruction-following examples (VLR-IF) designed to enhance RAG capabilities of VLMs. Evaluated on state-of-the-art models like Llava-Llama-3, it provides comprehensive assessment of multilingual visual question answering with retrieval augmentation across English, Korean, and Chinese.

description: In-context machine translation benchmark evaluating various large language models and specialized translation systems on multilingual translation tasks. The benchmark compares performance of instruction-tuned models like Mistral, Qwen, LLaMA, and Gemma against dedicated translation systems like Google Translate and NLLB across 28 languages. This comprehensive evaluation provides insights into the translation capabilities of general-purpose LLMs versus specialized translation models, covering a diverse range of languages from high-resource to low-resource settings and enabling assessment of cross-lingual transfer capabilities in translation tasks.

description: Breaking Language Barriers: A Question-Answering Dataset for Hindi and Marathi - largest SQuAD-style QA dataset for Hindi and Marathi with 28,000 samples each translated from SQuAD 2.0. Evaluates monolingual models (HindiBERT, MahaBERT) against multilingual models (mBERT, XLM-RoBERTa, DistilBERT). HindiBERT achieves best Hindi performance (47.84% EM), MahaBERT best for Marathi (42.97% EM). Uses novel sliding window technique for answer alignment in translated contexts. Demonstrates monolingual advantage over multilingual models for low-resource Indian languages.

description: HindiLLM: Large Language Model for Hindi - introduces two autoregressive Hindi language models (HindiLLM-Small: 124M parameters, HindiLLM-Medium: 355M parameters) trained from scratch on 37.34GB Hindi corpus. Custom HindiLLM tokenizer with 50K vocabulary reduces token fertility by 50% compared to GPT-2. Evaluated on 7 downstream tasks: sentiment analysis, text classification, NLI, CSQA, WSTP, discourse classification, machine translation. HindiLLM-Medium outperforms GPT-3.5 Turbo and other baselines across most tasks. Achieves 81.04% accuracy on IITP-Movie sentiment, 98.6% F1 on NLI.

description: MMCricBench-3K: Mind the Language Gap - Visual Question Answering on cricket scorecards evaluating Large Vision-Language Models on complex numerical and cross-lingual reasoning over semi-structured tabular images. Dataset comprises 1,463 synthetically generated scorecard images with 1,500 English QA pairs across three categories: Direct Retrieval (C1), Basic Arithmetic (C2), Multi-step Reasoning (C3). MMCricBench-E-1.5K (English) and MMCricBench-H-1.5K (Hindi) subsets reveal significant cross-lingual performance gaps. GPT-4o achieves best performance (57.3% English, 45.1% Hindi), demonstrating persistent challenges in structure-aware visual text understanding and cross-script generalization.

description: A Unified Framework for Multilingual and Code-Mixed Visual Question Answering (Gupta et al., AACL 2020) - First VQA framework for Hindi, English, and Code-mixed (Hinglish) languages. Introduces hierarchy of shared layers controlled by attention-based soft layer sharing mechanism. Uses bi-linear attention with residual connection to fuse language and image features. Creates Hindi and Code-mixed VQA datasets by exploiting linguistic properties. Achieves state-of-the-art performance across English, Hindi, and Code-mixed settings, demonstrating foundational experimental setup for Indic VQA with explicit Devanagari/code-mix capabilities.

description: MTVQA: Benchmarking Multilingual Text-Centric Visual Question Answering - First multilingual text-centric VQA benchmark with native human expert annotations across 9 languages covering 8,794 images and 28,607 QA pairs. description: Large-scale culturally diverse multilingual VQA benchmark covering 10,374 questions across 30 countries and 31 languages with 13 scripts. Created through grassroots crowdsourcing with native speakers and cultural experts. Features 10 categories: Vehicles, Cooking/Food, People/Everyday Life, Sports, Plants/Animals, Objects/Clothing, Brands, Geography/Landmarks, Tradition/Art/History, Public Figures/Pop Culture. Includes both personal and web-sourced images with cultural relevance validation. Demonstrates significant performance challenges for current MLLMs, especially in local languages. GPT-4o achieves highest accuracy (75.4% English, 74.3% local), while open models like LLaVA-1.5-7B achieve 49.6% English, 35.5% local languages. Shows substantial performance gaps between closed and open-source models, with particular difficulties in cooking/food and pop culture categories.

description: Largest publicly available multilingual context-grounded question-answering dataset for 11 major Indian languages from two language families. Encompasses both extractive and abstractive QA tasks incorporating existing datasets and English QA datasets translated into Indian languages. Synthetic dataset generated using Gemini model with manual verification. Combines Hindi SQuAD (5000 instances), XQuAD (1190 QA pairs), ChaII Dataset (1104 questions), IndicQA dataset (cloze-style reading comprehension), MLQA (4918 triples), MS MARCO (55k instances), NQ-Open, XORQA, LLaMA Index. Translate-Test paradigm significantly outperforms multilingual LLMs in low-resource languages. Evaluation using F1 scores for extractive QA and ROUGE-L for abstractive QA. Gemma2-Base performed best across most languages. BloomZ showed highest improvement for instruction-tuned models. Performance varies by resource availability: mid-resource languages (Hindi, Bengali) benefit from multilingual models while low-resource languages (Punjabi, Gujarati, Odia) perform better with translation-based approaches.

description: IndicMMLU-Pro is a comprehensive benchmark adapting MMLU-Pro for 9 major Indic languages using IndicTrans2 translation. Evaluates language understanding, reasoning, and generation across Indo-Aryan (Hindi, Bengali, Punjabi, Marathi, Urdu, Gujarati) and Dravidian (Telugu, Tamil, Kannada) languages. GPT-4o achieved highest performance (38.46-44.80%), followed by GPT-4o-mini (25.75-35.08%). Among specialized models, XLM-RoBERTa performed best (11.92-13.16%). Dataset quality verified through back-translation with chrF++ scores 74-78, BLEU 0.54-0.59. Covers diverse domains with rigorous quality assurance including expert proofreading by 13 native speakers. IndicMMLU-Pro is a comprehensive multitask benchmark for evaluating Large Language Models across 9 Indic languages, built upon MMLU-Pro framework. Covers language comprehension, reasoning, and generation tasks using high-quality IndicTrans2 translations with rigorous quality assurance. Baseline results show GPT-4o achieving 38.46%-44.80% accuracy, with significant performance gaps between advanced and specialized models highlighting need for Indic-specific AI development

description: IndicGenBench is the largest generation benchmark for 29 Indic languages spanning 13 scripts and 4 language families. Covers 5 user-facing tasks: cross-lingual summarization (CrossSum-In), machine translation (Flores-In), multilingual QA (XQuAD-In), cross-lingual QA (XorQA-In-Xx, XorQA-In-En). Languages categorized as higher resource (9), medium resource (7), and low resource (13). PaLM-2-L achieved best performance across most tasks, with significant performance gaps between English and Indic languages. Human-curated translations with professional annotators. Fine-tuning vs few-shot evaluation shows task-dependent preferences. Includes tokenizer fertility analysis showing 4.1-19.9 subwords per word across languages. IndicGenBench is the largest benchmark for evaluating text generation capabilities of multilingual models across 29 Indic languages spanning 13 scripts and 4 language families. The benchmark includes sentiment classification tasks covering cross-lingual summarization, machine translation, and question answering across Higher (Bengali, Hindi, Tamil, etc.), Medium (Assamese, Nepali, Punjabi, etc.), and Low resource (Awadhi, Manipuri, Santali, etc.) language categories. IndicGenBench provides comprehensive evaluation for GPT, PaLM-2, LLaMA, Gemma, and other major language models with significant performance gaps between English and Indic languages.

description: Cross-lingual adversarial dataset for paraphrase identification with 23,659 human-translated evaluation pairs across 6 typologically distinct languages. Generated from PAWS (Paraphrase Adversaries from Word Scrambling) using word swapping and back translation methods. Focuses on challenging paraphrase pairs with high lexical overlap but different semantic meanings. Multilingual BERT fine-tuned on English PAWS plus machine-translated data achieves 83.1-90.8% accuracy across non-English languages. PAWS-X is a cross-lingual adversarial dataset for paraphrase identification consisting of 23,659 human-translated PAWS evaluation pairs across six typologically distinct languages: French, Spanish, German, Chinese, Japanese, and Korean. The dataset addresses the gap in adversarial data generation beyond English by providing challenging paraphrase identification pairs that test models' capacity to capture non-local context and sentence structure. PAWS-X demonstrates the effectiveness of deep multilingual pre-training while leaving considerable headroom for multilingual research that better captures structural and contextual information.

description: First taxonomy-guided evaluation of Swahili NLP addressing sociolinguistic diversity. Dataset of 2,170 free-text responses from Kenyan speakers across 4 health-related psychometric tasks: Subjective Health Literacy, Trust in Doctors, Anxiety Visiting Doctor, Health Numeracy. Features tribal influences, urban vernacular (Sheng), code-mixing, and loanwords from 40+ ethnolinguistic communities. Benchmarked using TF-IDF baselines, pretrained language models, and instruction-tuned LLMs. Includes fairness evaluation across tribal, gender, age, education, and income dimensions using xAUC, Disparate Impact, and Fairness Violation metrics.

Includes explicit Telugu benchmarks for news and hybrid tasks. IndicBERT and MuRIL perform best. Analysis of LLMs and resource disparities. IndicGLUE: Large-scale Indic natural language understanding benchmark covering 11 languages including Telugu with tasks like News Category Classification, Headline Prediction, Wikipedia Section-title Prediction, Multiple-choice QA, NER and Cross-lingual Sentence Retrieval. Evaluates wide range of models from mBERT to GPT-4 with IndicBERT performing best on most tasks.

description: XNLI (Cross-lingual Natural Language Inference) is a benchmark for evaluating cross-lingual natural language inference capabilities across 15 languages. The dataset extends the MultiNLI corpus to include diverse languages including low-resource ones like Swahili and Urdu. XNLI enables evaluation of models' ability to understand entailment relationships between sentence pairs across different languages, providing both machine translation baselines and direct cross-lingual transfer evaluation. The benchmark includes premise-hypothesis pairs labeled as entailment, contradiction, or neutral, making it crucial for assessing multilingual reasoning capabilities in NLI tasks. Cross-lingual Natural Language Inference corpus with 5,000 test and 2,500 dev pairs translated from MultiNLI into 14 languages plus English (15 total). Evaluates textual entailment across languages with 392,702 training examples per language. Standard benchmark for cross-lingual sentence representations and multilingual NLI evaluation, widely used for assessing zero-shot cross-lingual transfer capabilities

description: MLDoc is a corpus for multilingual document classification across eight languages covering diverse linguistic families. The benchmark addresses cross-lingual document classification by creating a balanced subset of the Reuters corpus with uniform class distributions across languages including English, German, French, Spanish, Italian, Russian, Japanese, and Chinese. MLDoc provides strong baselines using multilingual word and sentence embeddings for all language transfer directions, enabling evaluation of cross-lingual transfer learning capabilities in document classification tasks across typologically diverse languages.

description: MASSIVE (Multilingual Amazon Slu resource package for Slot-filling, Intent classification, and Virtual assistant Evaluation) contains 1M realistic, parallel, labeled virtual assistant utterances spanning 51 typologically diverse languages from 29 genera. description: XTREME (Cross-lingual TRansfer Evaluation of Multilingual Encoders) is a comprehensive multi-task benchmark for evaluating cross-lingual generalization capabilities of multilingual representations across 40 languages and 9 tasks. The benchmark addresses the gap in multilingual model evaluation by providing diverse tasks including classification, structured prediction, question answering, and sentence retrieval. XTREME demonstrates significant performance gaps between English and cross-lingual transfer, particularly on syntactic and sentence retrieval tasks, with wide result spreads across languages, encouraging research on cross-lingual learning methods.

description: XGLUE is a comprehensive benchmark dataset for cross-lingual pre-training, understanding, and generation that extends beyond English-only evaluation. The benchmark provides 11 diversified tasks covering both natural language understanding and generation scenarios with labeled data in multiple languages. XGLUE enables training and evaluation of large-scale cross-lingual pre-trained models using multilingual and bilingual corpora, comparing models like Unicoder, Multilingual BERT, XLM, and XLM-R across diverse cross-lingual tasks and providing a more comprehensive evaluation framework than English-only benchmarks. XGLUE News Classification is a subset of the comprehensive XGLUE benchmark that evaluates cross-lingual pre-training, understanding, and generation across 19 languages including Arabic, Bulgarian, German, and others. This news classification task assesses models' ability to categorize news articles across diverse languages and scripts, providing evaluation for Multilingual BERT, XLM, XLM-R, and Unicoder models in cross-lingual transfer scenarios. The benchmark supports both natural language understanding and generation with labeled data spanning multiple language families.

The benchmark reveals significant biases and inconsistent performance across models and languages, particularly highlighting challenges with ambiguous scenarios and providing methodology for automated sentiment analysis evaluation.

description: IndicMMLU-Pro is a comprehensive benchmark adapting MMLU-Pro for 9 major Indic languages using IndicTrans2 translation. Evaluates language understanding, reasoning, and generation across Indo-Aryan (Hindi, Bengali, Punjabi, Marathi, Urdu, Gujarati) and Dravidian (Telugu, Tamil, Kannada) languages. GPT-4o achieved highest performance (38.46-44.80%), followed by GPT-4o-mini (25.75-35.08%). Among specialized models, XLM-RoBERTa performed best (11.92-13.16%). Dataset quality verified through back-translation with chrF++ scores 74-78, BLEU 0.54-0.59. Covers diverse domains with rigorous quality assurance including expert proofreading by 13 native speakers. IndicMMLU-Pro is a comprehensive multitask benchmark for evaluating Large Language Models across 9 Indic languages, built upon MMLU-Pro framework. Covers language comprehension, reasoning, and generation tasks using high-quality IndicTrans2 translations with rigorous quality assurance. Baseline results show GPT-4o achieving 38.46%-44.80% accuracy, with significant performance gaps between advanced and specialized models highlighting need for Indic-specific AI development

description: Cross-lingual adversarial dataset for paraphrase identification with 23,659 human-translated evaluation pairs across 6 typologically distinct languages. Generated from PAWS (Paraphrase Adversaries from Word Scrambling) using word swapping and back translation methods. Focuses on challenging paraphrase pairs with high lexical overlap but different semantic meanings. Multilingual BERT fine-tuned on English PAWS plus machine-translated data achieves 83.1-90.8% accuracy across non-English languages. PAWS-X is a cross-lingual adversarial dataset for paraphrase identification consisting of 23,659 human-translated PAWS evaluation pairs across six typologically distinct languages: French, Spanish, German, Chinese, Japanese, and Korean. The dataset addresses the gap in adversarial data generation beyond English by providing challenging paraphrase identification pairs that test models' capacity to capture non-local context and sentence structure. PAWS-X demonstrates the effectiveness of deep multilingual pre-training while leaving considerable headroom for multilingual research that better captures structural and contextual information.

description: SAHARA: Most comprehensive benchmark for African NLP covering 517 languages across 50 African countries. description: XGLUE is a comprehensive benchmark dataset for cross-lingual pre-training, understanding, and generation that extends beyond English-only evaluation. The benchmark provides 11 diversified tasks covering both natural language understanding and generation scenarios with labeled data in multiple languages. XGLUE enables training and evaluation of large-scale cross-lingual pre-trained models using multilingual and bilingual corpora, comparing models like Unicoder, Multilingual BERT, XLM, and XLM-R across diverse cross-lingual tasks and providing a more comprehensive evaluation framework than English-only benchmarks. XGLUE News Classification is a subset of the comprehensive XGLUE benchmark that evaluates cross-lingual pre-training, understanding, and generation across 19 languages including Arabic, Bulgarian, German, and others. This news classification task assesses models' ability to categorize news articles across diverse languages and scripts, providing evaluation for Multilingual BERT, XLM, XLM-R, and Unicoder models in cross-lingual transfer scenarios. The benchmark supports both natural language understanding and generation with labeled data spanning multiple language families.

description: MLSUM: The Multilingual Summarization Corpus (EMNLP 2020): The first large-scale MultiLingual SUMmarization dataset obtained from online newspapers, containing 1.5M+ article/summary pairs across French, German, Spanish, Russian, and Turkish languages. Combined with English data from CNN/Daily Mail, MLSUM forms a comprehensive multilingual dataset enabling new research directions in text summarization. The benchmark provides cross-lingual comparative analyses using state-of-the-art systems including Oracle, Random, Lead-3, TextRank, Pointer-Generator, M-BERT, and mBERT models, highlighting existing biases and demonstrating the importance of multilingual evaluation in summarization research. MLSUM addresses the gap in large-scale multilingual summarization resources and motivates development of more inclusive summarization models.

description: Comprehensive Russian-language multimodal dataset for automatic summarization of scientific papers containing 420 papers across 7 domains (Economics, History, Information Technologies, Journalism, Law, Linguistics, Medicine). The dataset includes textual content, tables, and figures from Russian scientific journals (Vestnik TSU, Vestnik NSU, Medicinsky journal). Evaluation used Gigachat (SBER) and YandexGPT models with metrics including BERTScore, BLEURT, ROUGE-1/2/L, and BLEU. Results showed YandexGPT achieving better performance with BERTScore 0.692 and ROUGE-1 0.118, while Gigachat achieved BERTScore 0.719 but processed fewer texts (37% vs 70%) due to ethical restrictions. Dataset publicly available on GitHub for multimodal scientific text summarization research.

models: Qwen2.5-7B, Jirai-Qwen (fine-tuned), Llama-3.1-8B, DeepSeek-v3
description: JiraiBench introduces the first bilingual benchmark evaluating LLMs' effectiveness in detecting self-destructive content across Chinese and Japanese social media communities. The dataset comprises 10,419 Chinese posts and 5,000 Japanese posts from Jirai community spanning three behavioral categories: drug overdose (OD), eating disorders (ED), and self-harm (SH). Models evaluated include Qwen2.5-7B, Jirai-Qwen (fine-tuned on Chinese data), Llama-3.1-8B, and DeepSeek-v3. Experiments revealed significant cross-cultural transfer effects where Japanese instruction prompts outperformed Chinese prompts when processing Chinese content, suggesting cultural proximity can outweigh linguistic similarity. Zero-shot approaches consistently outperformed few-shot methods. Evaluation used Macro-F1 scores across multidimensional annotation scheme. The fine-tuned Jirai-Qwen achieved highest performance on Chinese data, and cross-lingual transfer experiments demonstrated knowledge transfer between Chinese and Japanese without explicit target language training.

description: Comprehensive meta-analysis examining over 2,000 multilingual benchmarks from 148 countries (2021-2024). Reveals persistent language disparities with English overrepresented despite exclusion from collection. Study covers text classification (66.5%), question answering, summarization, and other NLP tasks across news (17%), social media (13.3%), Wikipedia (9.6%), and specialized domains. Key findings: STEM tasks (ARC, MGSM) show stronger correlation with human judgments (0.70-0.85) than NLP tasks like XQuAD (0.11-0.30). Localized benchmarks (CMMLU) significantly outperform translated equivalents in human alignment (0.68 vs 0.47). Estimated $11M investment in benchmark development. Advocates for culturally authentic, linguistically diverse evaluation beyond translation-based approaches.
embedding_text:
Description: Comprehensive meta-analysis examining over 2,000 multilingual benchmarks from 148 countries (2021-2024). Reveals persistent language disparities with English overrepresented despite exclusion from collection. Study covers text classification (66.5%), question answering, summarization, and other NLP tasks across news (17%), social media (13.3%), Wikipedia (9.6%), and specialized domains. Key findings: STEM tasks (ARC, MGSM) show stronger correlation with human judgments (0.70-0.85) than NLP tasks like XQuAD (0.11-0.30). Localized benchmarks (CMMLU) significantly outperform translated equivalents in human alignment (0.68 vs 0.47). Estimated $11M investment in benchmark development. Advocates for culturally authentic, linguistically diverse evaluation beyond translation-based approaches.

description: Investigation of LLMs' struggle with basic character counting tasks (e.g., counting 'r' in 'strawberry'). Evaluates multiple advanced LLMs including GPT-4o, Llama-3, Qwen-1.5, Gemma-1, Phi-3, Mistral-v0.3, DeepSeek-V2, InternLM, and Yi-1.5. Tests various conjectures about tokenization, architecture, and training data as causes for counting failures. Demonstrates that engaging reasoning mechanisms is most robust approach for improving performance. Challenges prevalent assumptions about inherent LLM deficiencies and highlights importance of reasoning-before-responding during model training. Published at NAACL 2025.

description: La Leaderboard: The first open-source leaderboard evaluating generative large language models in languages and language varieties of Spain and Latin America, including Spanish, Catalan, Basque, and Galician. This comprehensive multilingual evaluation framework evaluates 50 LLMs across 66 datasets. Includes text summarization tasks using NoticIA (Spanish), caBreu (Catalan), and Summarization-GL (Galician) datasets. Evaluates models using BLEU, ROUGE metrics with few-shot configurations. Top performers: Gemma-2-9B (61.69% ES, 57.30% CA), Qwen2.5-32B-IT (64.06% ES), Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-IT (59.03% ES, 57.01% CA). The benchmark showcases evaluation results of models including Gemma2, LLaMA 3.1, Qwen2.5, EuroLLM, and Salamandra variants. Energy efficiency analysis included with 92.09 kg CO2 emissions for full evaluation. La Leaderboard provides methodology guidance for selecting suitable evaluation setups for downstream tasks, advocating for fewer few-shot examples to reduce environmental impact and facilitate reproducible results for broader research communities, specifically targeting Spanish varieties and co-official languages of Spain.

description: Comprehensive human evaluation study of 10 LLMs across different pretraining methods, prompts, and model scales for news summarization. Key findings: (1) Instruction tuning, not model size, is crucial for zero-shot summarization capability; (2) Previous studies underestimated human performance due to low-quality references. Conducted human evaluation using high-quality summaries from freelance writers. Found LLM summaries comparable to human-written summaries despite stylistic differences in paraphrasing. Evaluated on CNN/DailyMail and XSUM datasets. Demonstrates importance of instruction tuning for summarization tasks and challenges existing evaluation paradigms with better human baselines.

description: PublicHearingBR: First long-document summarization benchmark for Brazilian Portuguese. Contains 206 pairs of public hearing transcripts (~18k words each, up to 147k words max) from Brazilian Chamber of Deputies with corresponding news articles and structured summaries. Evaluates ChatGPT, GPT-4o, GPT-4o mini, DeepSeek-V3, and Sabiá-3.1 on opinion extraction task. Results: 45% recall, 25% precision, 12% hallucination rate. Includes 4,238 manually annotated opinion-text pairs for NLI evaluation. Uses hybrid summarization approach with custom prompts for extracting individuals and their opinions. Addresses gap in Portuguese long-document summarization datasets. Comprehensive evaluation methodology including automatic hallucination detection using LLM-as-judge approach with vector similarity retrieval.

description: XL-Sum: Largest multilingual abstractive summarization dataset with 1 million professionally annotated article-summary pairs from BBC across 44 languages. Covers low to high-resource languages with highly abstractive, concise summaries. Portuguese: 71k+ pairs (ROUGE-1=37.17, ROUGE-L=28.56). Swahili: 9,872 pairs (ROUGE-1=37.67, ROUGE-2=17.85, ROUGE-L=30.91). Tamil: 20k+ pairs (ROUGE-1=24.33, ROUGE-2=11.06, ROUGE-L=22.07). Fine-tuned mT5 achieving >11 ROUGE-2 scores on 10 languages, with some exceeding 15 through multilingual training. Demonstrates competitive performance for low-resource summarization. Standard benchmark for multilingual news summarization research. XL-Sum addresses the limited availability of datasets for low/mid-resource languages in text summarization, featuring highly abstractive, concise, and high-quality summaries validated through human and intrinsic evaluation. The benchmark demonstrates competitive performance with mT5 fine-tuning, achieving over 11 ROUGE-2 scores across 10 languages and exceeding 15 on some through multilingual training.

description: Comprehensive systematic survey of Multilingual Large Language Models covering evaluation benchmarks including XL-Sum, SEAHORSE, and WikiLingua datasets which include Portuguese and Russian languages. Survey analyzes models including GPT-3/4, Mistral, Qwen, DeepSeek, Mixtral, and BLOOM across diverse evaluation frameworks. SEAHORSE dataset provides multilingual summarization evaluation with human ratings across 6 languages including Portuguese, with 96K summaries from multiple models. WikiLingua supports multilingual Wikipedia summarization including Portuguese and Russian. Research covers 122+ languages across various language families with comprehensive evaluation of multilingual capabilities, safety, and performance across high-resource and low-resource languages including detailed analysis of summarization task performance.

description: Comprehensive bias and fairness audit of Swahili NLP with novel annotated dataset of 2,170 health-related text responses from Kenyan speakers. Research evaluates models including Qwen2.5-7B/72B, LLaMA3.1, AfriBERTa, mBERT, and XLM-RoBERTa across health psychometric tasks (Literacy, Trust, Anxiety, Numeracy). Results show significant performance gaps compared to English: AUC scores 20-30% lower than English BERT baseline, with AfriBERTa achieving 0.604 literacy AUC, Qwen2.5-72B achieving 0.546 literacy AUC. Dataset captures sociolinguistic variation including Sheng (urban vernacular), code-mixing, tribal lexicons, and loanwords across 40+ ethnolinguistic communities. Systematic error analysis reveals linguistic features like Sheng and tribal terms drive mispredictions, with intersectional bias compounding across demographic dimensions (tribe, gender, education, income).

description: WikiLingua: Large-scale multilingual benchmark dataset for cross-lingual abstractive summarization extracted from WikiHow collaborative how-to guides across 18 languages including English, Spanish, Portuguese, French, German, Russian, Italian, Indonesian, Dutch, Arabic, Vietnamese, Chinese, Thai, Japanese, Korean, Hindi, Czech, and Turkish. Dataset features gold-standard article-summary alignments across languages using image alignment methodology. Research evaluates cross-lingual summarization approaches including direct crosslingual methods without translation at inference time, leveraging synthetic data and Neural Machine Translation for pre-training. Baseline comparisons include traditional pipeline approaches (Summarize→Translate, Translate→Summarize) and advanced multilingual models (mT5, mBART-50) with both monolingual and cross-lingual fine-tuning strategies. WikiLingua Enhanced provides enhanced methodologies for cross-lingual transfer learning, evaluating various approaches including fine-tuned mBART models, Lead-3 baselines, and pipeline methods across multiple language pairs. The study demonstrates improved performance through enhanced cross-lingual training techniques, addressing challenges in cross-lingual summarization such as maintaining content quality across language boundaries while ensuring semantic preservation and factual accuracy. Evaluation uses automatic metrics (ROUGE, BLEU) with human evaluation studies, demonstrating significant performance improvements for direct cross-lingual approaches while maintaining cost efficiency during inference.

description: Global Voices is a multilingual dataset for evaluating cross-lingual summarization methods across 15 languages, extracting social-network descriptions of Global Voices news articles to collect evaluation data for into-English and from-English summarization tasks. The study constructs a high-quality evaluation dataset based on guidelines emphasizing accuracy, coverage, and understandability, with crowd-sourced summaries filtered through human ratings. The benchmark examines translation quality effects in cross-lingual summarization, comparing translate-then-summarize approaches with various baselines. Results highlight limitations of the ROUGE metric overlooked in monolingual summarization, providing insights into cross-lingual summarization challenges and evaluation methodologies.

description: CrossSum is a large-scale cross-lingual summarization dataset comprising 1.68 million article-summary samples across 1,500+ language pairs, representing the largest cross-lingual summarization dataset and the first not centered around English. The benchmark creates CrossSum by aligning parallel articles across languages via cross-lingual retrieval from multilingual abstractive summarization datasets, validated through controlled human evaluation. The study proposes a multistage data sampling algorithm for training cross-lingual summarization models capable of summarizing articles in any target language, introducing LaSE (embedding-based metric) for automatic evaluation that correlates strongly with ROUGE while enabling reliable measurement without target language references.

description: GlobeSumm is a challenging benchmark that unifies multi-lingual, cross-lingual, and multi-document news summarization (MCMS) to address real-world scenarios where news about international events involves multiple documents in different languages. The benchmark meticulously constructs the GLOBESUMM dataset by collecting multilingual news reports and restructuring them into event-centric format, introducing protocol-guided prompting for high-quality and cost-effective reference annotation. GlobeSumm highlights challenges of conflicts between news reports, redundancies, and omissions, evaluating models like GPT-3.5-turbo-16k, Vicuna-7B, and ChatGLM3 across diverse languages including Albanian, Arabic, Armenian, and others, demonstrating the complexity of multilingual summarization in real-world scenarios.

description: MLMD-news is a mixed-language multi-document news summarization dataset addressing real-world scenarios where international events involve multiple documents in different languages. The benchmark contains four different languages with 10,992 source document cluster and target summary pairs, filling the gap in mixed-language multi-document (MLMD) summarization research. The study proposes a graph-based extract-generate model evaluating various approaches including Centroid+mT5, mBART, GPT-3.5, GPT-4, LLaMA-3.1, and Gemini-1.5 across English, German, French, and Spanish. This work advances research in summarization scenarios involving multiple languages within single document clusters, providing benchmarks and methodologies for complex multilingual summarization tasks.

description: SITR (Summarization, Improvement, Translation, Refinement) is a four-step zero-shot method for cross-lingual summarization in low-resource languages, unlocking the potential of large language models through meta-generation strategies. The benchmark evaluates mBART-50, mT5 variants, GPT models, LLaMA3, Gemma2, Mixtral, and Qwen-1.5 across 18 languages including Ukrainian, Bengali, Gujarati, and others. SITR significantly outperforms traditional few-shot learning and summarize-translate approaches, with GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 achieving substantial improvements in ROUGE scores (78-103% increases) and BERTScore metrics. The method demonstrates that current LLMs can effectively handle cross-lingual summarization for low-resource languages through strategic prompting and iterative refinement processes.

description: MLSUM: The Multilingual Summarization Corpus (EMNLP 2020): The first large-scale MultiLingual SUMmarization dataset obtained from online newspapers, containing 1.5M+ article/summary pairs across French, German, Spanish, Russian, and Turkish languages. Combined with English data from CNN/Daily Mail, MLSUM forms a comprehensive multilingual dataset enabling new research directions in text summarization. The benchmark provides cross-lingual comparative analyses using state-of-the-art systems including Oracle, Random, Lead-3, TextRank, Pointer-Generator, M-BERT, and mBERT models, highlighting existing biases and demonstrating the importance of multilingual evaluation in summarization research. MLSUM addresses the gap in large-scale multilingual summarization resources and motivates development of more inclusive summarization models.

description: JiraiBench introduces the first bilingual benchmark evaluating LLMs' effectiveness in detecting self-destructive content across Chinese and Japanese social media communities. The dataset comprises 10,419 Chinese posts and 5,000 Japanese posts from Jirai community spanning three behavioral categories: drug overdose (OD), eating disorders (ED), and self-harm (SH). Models evaluated include Qwen2.5-7B, Jirai-Qwen (fine-tuned on Chinese data), Llama-3.1-8B, and DeepSeek-v3. Experiments revealed significant cross-cultural transfer effects where Japanese instruction prompts outperformed Chinese prompts when processing Chinese content, suggesting cultural proximity can outweigh linguistic similarity. Zero-shot approaches consistently outperformed few-shot methods. Evaluation used Macro-F1 scores across multidimensional annotation scheme. The fine-tuned Jirai-Qwen achieved highest performance on Chinese data, and cross-lingual transfer experiments demonstrated knowledge transfer between Chinese and Japanese without explicit target language training.

description: Comprehensive meta-analysis examining over 2,000 multilingual benchmarks from 148 countries (2021-2024). Reveals persistent language disparities with English overrepresented despite exclusion from collection. Study covers text classification (66.5%), question answering, summarization, and other NLP tasks across news (17%), social media (13.3%), Wikipedia (9.6%), and specialized domains. Key findings: STEM tasks (ARC, MGSM) show stronger correlation with human judgments (0.70-0.85) than NLP tasks like XQuAD (0.11-0.30). Localized benchmarks (CMMLU) significantly outperform translated equivalents in human alignment (0.68 vs 0.47). Estimated $11M investment in benchmark development. Advocates for culturally authentic, linguistically diverse evaluation beyond translation-based approaches.
